title: Kulcha

overview.description: A soft, leavened North Indian flatbread with a lightly crisp, blistered surface, cooked on a very hot tawa or heavy frying pan.

ingredients: Melted butter or ghee, for brushing
method: Mix the flour, baking powder, baking soda, sugar and salt in a large bowl.
Gradually add the warm water, mixing until a soft dough forms.
Knead for 5–8 minutes until smooth and pliable.
Cover and rest for at least 1 hour.
Divide the dough into 4 equal portions and roll each into a smooth ball.
Flatten one ball and roll it into a thick, roughly circular disc.
Sprinkle with fresh coriander if using and press it gently into the surface.
Heat a heavy-based frying pan or cast-iron skillet over high heat until very hot.
Place the kulcha in the dry pan.
Cook until bubbles appear and the underside develops browned spots.
Turn and cook the other side until browned and cooked through.
Brush with melted butter or ghee.

learning.did-you-know: Kulcha is strongly associated with Punjab, particularly Amritsar, and is traditionally cooked in a tandoor. It may be made plain or stuffed with ingredients such as potato or onion.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How yogurt, chemical leavening and high heat create a soft, blistered flatbread without requiring yeast.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A useful addition to the Indian bread collection that demonstrates how yogurt and chemical leavening can create a soft, blistered bread without yeast.
chapman-tips: The dough should be soft and pliable rather than stiff.
Resting the dough gives the flour time to hydrate and makes shaping easier.
Get the pan properly hot before cooking the kulcha.
Avoid cooking too many at once; the pan needs to stay hot.
Don't press the kulcha down while it is cooking or you may prevent it from puffing.
Brush with ghee or butter immediately after cooking for the best flavour.
For a more traditional flavour, use a cast-iron pan if you have one.
notes.batch-cooking: Cooked kulcha can be cooled, wrapped and frozen. Reheat in a dry frying pan or hot oven.
notes.leftovers: Use leftover kulcha to scoop up curry, make a sandwich or serve with eggs.
